Case title: Objective party-affiliation annotation across 11 countries
Short description: Use politician social-media posts with known party labels to test when human coders can be demoted from primary coders to auditors.
Empirical unit of analysis: Social-media posts paired with public party labels across 11 countries and languages
Method traditions: Computational text analysis; quantitative political science
Observable human actors: Expert coders, crowd workers, RA manager
Candidate roles tested: Construct boundary setter; shrinking routine coder
Key typology question: Is routine coding a shrinking task-cluster on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks?
Observable artifacts / traces: Prompt logs, coder disagreement, model outputs, downstream classification stability
Key outcomes observed: Accuracy, macro-F1, downstream inference stability, time saved
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Humans continue to outperform or materially change downstream conclusions on objective tasks
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Task is unusually objective; may not generalize to contested constructs
Why this case has high leverage: Public ground truth makes it one of the cleanest displacement tests available now.
main_limitations: Primary journal-page URL is access-limited, but a lawful open-access deposit (secondary source) covers the substantive evidence.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.

Case title: Contested codebook measurement of manifestos, protest events, and political violence
Short description: Compare AI-only, human-only, and hybrid codebook workflows on contested political-science constructs with hard negatives and transport tests.
Empirical unit of analysis: Texts plus formal political-science codebooks and adjudicated labels
Candidate roles tested: Construct boundary setter
Key typology question: Does human intervention change construct validity rather than only speed?
Observable artifacts / traces: Codebook versions, prompt histories, hard-negative sets, edge-case adjudications
Key outcomes observed: Out-of-domain transport, subgroup stability, edge-case behavior, validity of downstream measures
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: AI-only codebooks match human-revised codebooks on edge cases and transport
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Extra labels may be confounded with better construct work
Why this case has high leverage: This is the strongest direct test of a constitutive human role in social-science measurement.

Case title: Multi-perspective annotation of political incivility or offensiveness
Short description: Label the same texts by demographic or partisan subgroups and compare majority-vote, AI-only, and targeted human-sampling corrections.
Empirical unit of analysis: Texts with subgroup labels and matched AI proxy labels
Candidate roles tested: Construct boundary setter; perspective sampler
Key typology question: Should construct work be split from perspective sampling when disagreement is meaningful?
Observable artifacts / traces: Subgroup label distributions, active-learning logs, disagreement matrices
Key outcomes observed: Preservation of subgroup-specific inference, minority-label retention, annotation budget efficiency
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Majority-vote or AI-only labels preserve subgroup-specific inferences equally well
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Task underspecification may masquerade as genuine perspectival disagreement
Why this case has high leverage: It can force a split in the starting typology rather than merely confirm it.

Case title: Party-cue perturbation in political-text annotation
Short description: Hold policy text constant while varying party cues to test whether AI coding tracks politically loaded labels rather than substantive content.
Empirical unit of analysis: Identical policy statements with manipulated party labels
Candidate roles tested: Construct boundary setter; bias auditor
Key typology question: Is construct work separable from perspective-aware bias auditing?
Observable artifacts / traces: Cue-perturbation experiments, confidence shifts, divergence from human labels
Key outcomes observed: Sensitivity to party cues, asymmetry across parties, stability of labels under perturbation
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Cue changes do not systematically move outputs
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Party labels can sometimes be substantively relevant context rather than pure bias
Why this case has high leverage: It makes model-mediated political bias visible at the measurement stage.

Case title: Human-developed codebooks for policy stakeholder interview coding
Short description: Compare AI coding quality when the codebook is supplied by policy experts versus when AI proposes themes and labels first.
Empirical unit of analysis: Paragraphs from stakeholder interviews plus iterative codebooks and prompt histories
Candidate roles tested: Construct boundary setter; context witness; protocol architect

Key outcomes observed: Specificity of themes, policy usability, inter-method agreement, time saved
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: AI-first thematic analysis without human codebook development is equally specific and usable
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Policy interviews may be more structured than ethnographic or highly sensitive data
Why this case has high leverage: Visible codebook and revision traces make the role shift empirically inspectable.

Case title: Maternal-health interview coding with generative AI
Short description: Use semistructured maternal-health interviews to test what is gained and lost when GenAI performs inductive coding and theme extraction.
Empirical unit of analysis: Interview transcripts from maternity-care providers
Candidate roles tested: Construct boundary setter; situated interlocutor; context witness

Observable artifacts / traces: Structured prompts, generated themes, human–AI code comparisons, coding memos
Key outcomes observed: Theme overlap, coding efficiency, missing contextual nuance, interpretive adequacy
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: LLM coding captures nuance and downstream interpretive usefulness as well as human coding
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Applied health interviews may not generalize to more theory-laden or politically contested domains
Why this case has high leverage: Useful counterpoint to political-text cases because the topic is applied, interview-based, and semi-structured.

Case title: Agentic title/abstract screening in migration-policy reviews with gray-literature rescue
Short description: Test AI-first screening on mixed corpora to see whether first-pass screening shrinks while protocol design and corpus rescue remain human.
Empirical unit of analysis: Review records, inclusion decisions, and rescue decisions across indexed and gray sources
Observable human actors: Review lead, RA screeners, librarian-equivalent search designer
Candidate roles tested: Protocol and provenance architect; corpus pluralist; shrinking first-pass screener
Key typology question: Has first-pass screening become a shrinking task-cluster rather than a durable role?
Observable artifacts / traces: PRISMA logs, search paths, inclusion/exclusion reasons, rescued-study logs
Key outcomes observed: Recall, precision, workload saved, conclusion sensitivity to rescued studies
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: AI-first workflows preserve gray and multilingual evidence without human rescue
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Access to subscriptions and topic difficulty can mimic AI effects
Why this case has high leverage: It tests both shrinkage and durable boundary-setting in one workflow.

Case title: Study-type classification and review triage for social-policy evidence synthesis
Short description: Benchmark specialized classifiers and LLMs for study-type classification in reviews where social-policy evidence mixes methods and weak metadata.
Empirical unit of analysis: Abstracts and study records from review corpora
Candidate roles tested: Corpus pluralist; protocol architect

Key outcomes observed: Sensitivity/specificity by source type, triage speed, error concentration
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Predictive triage works equally well on heterogeneous and poorly structured sources
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Benchmark corpora may be cleaner than actual policy-review inputs

Case title: Citation-grounded multi-paper synthesis for social-policy literatures
Short description: Use retrieval-augmented synthesis to generate evidence maps or literature-survey memos from a fixed social-policy corpus with citation checking.
Empirical unit of analysis: Literature-survey memos generated from fixed corpora plus citation logs
Candidate roles tested: Protocol and provenance architect; corpus pluralist; accountability and labor allocator

Observable artifacts / traces: Citation logs, retrieval snippets, unsupported-claim checks, human correction records
Key outcomes observed: Citation accuracy, unsupported claims, source coverage, correction burden
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Grounded and non-grounded systems perform equally well on claim support and coverage
Main confounds / risks of misreading: OpenScholar is not social-science-specific, so transfer to policy literatures must be verified

Case title: Citation-hallucination checks in AI-assisted literature-review drafts
Short description: Track when humans verify or fail to verify citations inserted by AI during literature-review drafting and evidence synthesis.
Empirical unit of analysis: Draft review sections, bibliographies, and verification logs
Candidate roles tested: Accountability and labor allocator; protocol and provenance architect
Key typology question: Is provenance architecture distinct from accountability, or are they the same role in literature review practice?
Observable artifacts / traces: Draft histories, bibliography correction logs, fake-reference incidents, disclosure notes
Key outcomes observed: Citation error rates, verification burden, correction timing, responsibility assignment
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Citation support tools eliminate hallucination risk without extra human verification work
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Poor citation habits could predate AI and inflate apparent AI effects
Why this case has high leverage: This case can show whether two typology categories collapse in practice.

Case title: LLM-assisted grant proposal ideation in competitive funding calls
Short description: Compare AI-assisted and non-assisted proposal ideation under real funding calls to test whether AI changes what gets proposed, not just how it is written.
Empirical unit of analysis: Proposal abstracts, aims pages, ideation memos, and AI-use traces
Observable human actors: PI, funder-facing applicant, postdoc, doctoral student
Candidate roles tested: Relevance adjudicator; accountability and labor allocator
Key typology question: Does AI compress agendas toward institutionally legible questions?
Observable artifacts / traces: Draft histories, semantic-distinctiveness scores, reviewer comments, AI-use logs
Key outcomes observed: Novelty, semantic distinctiveness, stakeholder relevance, funding outcomes
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: AI use leaves distinctiveness and stakeholder relevance unchanged or improves both
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Austerity, field maturity, and call design can compress agendas even without AI
Why this case has high leverage: Without a funding case, relevance adjudication remains underspecified and too abstract.

Case title: Funding-pipeline agenda compression under LLM-assisted proposal writing
Short description: Link inferred AI involvement in submitted proposals to semantic distinctiveness, reviewer reactions, and downstream publication novelty.
Empirical unit of analysis: Proposal texts, award records, downstream publication traces
Candidate roles tested: Relevance adjudicator; accountability and labor allocator
Key typology question: Is relevance adjudication a robust role or mainly an institution-specific pressure point?
Observable artifacts / traces: Proposal text corpora, semantic embeddings, award outcomes, reviewer reports
Key outcomes observed: Distinctiveness, success rate, long-run impact, reviewer language
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Higher AI involvement predicts equal or greater long-run novelty
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Causal inference is hard because ambitious labs may both use AI and write better proposals
Why this case has high leverage: It converts abstract worries about agenda compression into observable portfolio-level evidence.

Case title: Scideator-style facet recombination for social-science prospectus ideation

Case title: ResearchBench / DiscoveryBench sociology and economics discovery tasks
Short description: Use benchmark tasks drawn from sociology and economics to test whether AI supports inspiration retrieval, hypothesis composition, or ranking differently.

Case title: Multi-agent scientific idea generation as a boundary case for social-science agenda work
Short description: Treat agent teams that brainstorm, critique, and refine ideas as a boundary case for whether collaborative AI can displace human agenda setting.
Empirical unit of analysis: Generated idea portfolios and reviewer scores

Case title: AI conversational interviewing on welfare stigma or political fear
Short description: Compare AI-only, human-only, and human+AI interviewing on sensitive topics to test whether rapport, repair, and ethical pause remain constitutive.

Key outcomes observed: Disclosure depth, rapport, trust, mode effects, downstream interpretive utility

Case title: Dynamic Surveys with AI-generated follow-ups and cluster reports
Short description: Use dynamic surveys to test a middle ground between structured surveys and interviews, with AI-generated follow-ups and emergent cluster reports.

Case title: QDA delegation preferences: human-only versus human-initiated versus AI-initiated coding
Short description: Study how qualitative researchers choose among delegation levels in coding and thematic analysis.

Case title: AI-assisted memoing of ethnographic or historical fieldnotes

Case title: LLM-guided reflexive content analysis and codebook redesign

Case title: Synthetic respondents benchmarked against ANES/GSS/ESS

Case title: Fine-tuned SocSci210 human-behavior simulators

Case title: Forecasting outcomes before field experiments
Short description: Inject AI forecasts into preregistration or design workflows and observe which design decisions actually change.
Empirical unit of analysis: Preregistrations, forecast logs, design revisions, ex post results
Method traditions: Experimental economics; political science; policy evaluation
Candidate roles tested: Counterfactual and transportability judge
Key typology question: Does predictive success alter what is worth identifying, or only improve calibration?
Observable artifacts / traces: Forecast logs, redesign memos, preregistration diffs, final outcomes
Key outcomes observed: Calibration, novelty, theoretical sharpness, changed outcome measures or estimands

Case title: AI multiverse / nonstandard errors in autonomous empirical analysis
Short description: Run many autonomous agents on the same data and hypothesis to estimate nonstandard errors from model and sampling variation.
Empirical unit of analysis: Independent AI-agent reports, code, estimates, and revision-stage changes on identical tasks
Candidate roles tested: Counterfactual and transportability judge; protocol and provenance architect

Key outcomes observed: Dispersion of estimates, convergence after feedback, model-family preferences

Case title: Project APE end-to-end autonomous policy evaluation tournament
Short description: Use APE's public papers, matches, and audit materials to study what human roles remain when an agentic system writes empirical economics papers end-to-end.
Empirical unit of analysis: APE papers, tournament matches, code/data packages, and leaderboard records
Observable human actors: Human initializer, reviser, external critic, tournament designer

Observable artifacts / traces: Prompt seeds, leaderboard stats, paper versions, replication materials, public issue reports
Key outcomes observed: Autonomy gradient, paper quality signal, revision effects, integrity-check failure rates
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: APE outputs prove reliable enough that human boundary-setting and accountability no longer materially change use decisions
Main confounds / risks of misreading: APE's tournament is an internal quality proxy and may not map to real journal or policy standards

Case title: Deep Research agents for policy-literature reconnaissance
Short description: Adapt long-horizon web-research agents to frozen or live policy-literature tasks and examine when human source-boundary decisions still dominate.
Empirical unit of analysis: Long-horizon research traces, retrieved sources, and final policy-literature memos
Candidate roles tested: Corpus pluralist; protocol and provenance architect
Key typology question: Can agentic web research reduce human literature work without silently narrowing the evidentiary universe?
Observable artifacts / traces: Browser traces, source lists, hallucination markers, final memos
Key outcomes observed: Source diversity, claim grounding, retrieval depth, forgetting or drift
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Agentic research produces equally diverse and well-grounded corpora without human boundary setting
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Current deep-research benchmarks are not social-science-specific and often use frozen web corpora
Why this case has high leverage: Useful to stress-test corpus pluralism under live or semi-live web search conditions.

Case title: AgentSociety and generative social simulation as a transportability boundary case
Short description: Use large-scale LLM social simulation to test whether calibration and transportability become separate human roles when simulated societies are used for policy insight.
Empirical unit of analysis: Simulated agent interactions, policy interventions, and comparisons to real-world patterns
Observable human actors: Model builder, social scientist, policy analyst
Candidate roles tested: Calibration architect; counterfactual and transportability judge
Key typology question: Does simulation quality force a split between calibration work and broader transportability judgment?
Observable artifacts / traces: Simulation logs, parameter settings, validation comparisons, intervention outputs
Key outcomes observed: Alignment with real-world patterns, failure under distribution shift, policy relevance
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Simulation outputs transport reliably enough that calibration and transportability collapse into one role
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Agent-based simulations may look realistic while still missing institutional mechanisms
Why this case has high leverage: It is a strong pressure test for predictive-role splitting beyond surveys and experiments.

Case title: Doctoral-student AI use and training needs across dissertation workflows
Short description: Use doctoral-student AI adoption patterns to identify where training shifts from prompt fluency to provenance, validation, and calibration are most needed.
Empirical unit of analysis: Survey and interview data on doctoral students' AI-tool use
Candidate roles tested: Protocol and provenance architect; accountability and labor allocator
Key typology question: Are new researcher roles teachable and institutionally scaffolded, or only emergent in high-end labs?
Observable artifacts / traces: Survey responses, training logs, self-reported use by phase, perceived benefits/risks
Key outcomes observed: Phase-specific adoption, perceived skills gaps, governance expectations
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Prompt-centric training performs as well as provenance- and validation-centric training expectations
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Self-reports may not match actual workflow traces
Why this case has high leverage: Without a pedagogy case, the typology remains descriptive and misses role reproduction.

Case title: Methods-course experiment with predictive design tools and provenance logging
Fit to social science: Proposed design anchored in current evidence
Short description: Randomize doctoral or advanced methods students to standard design training versus predictive-AI-plus-provenance training to see which role distinctions are teachable.
Empirical unit of analysis: Student preregistrations, forecast logs, provenance records, rubric scores
Candidate roles tested: Counterfactual and transportability judge; calibration architect; protocol and provenance architect
Key typology question: Are calibration and counterfactual judgment distinct enough to teach and evaluate separately?
Observable artifacts / traces: Preregistration diffs, forecast logs, audit trails, grading rubrics, reflections
Key outcomes observed: Identification quality, calibration, over-trust, provenance completeness
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Students trained mainly in prompting perform just as well on identification and provenance tasks
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Artificial classroom settings may not map to real lab or dissertation pressures
Why this case has high leverage: This is the cleanest way to test whether the typology can structure training rather than only retrospective analysis.
Notes: Not yet directly studied in the specific configuration proposed.
